question.text: How are profits and losses from exchange-traded derivative transactions classified for income tax purposes in India?

answer.text: As non-speculative business income under Section 43(5) of the Income Tax Act, as amended by Finance Act 2005
As speculative income that can only be set off against other speculative gains
As capital gains subject to STCG/LTCG tax rates depending on holding period
As income from other sources, taxable at slab rates without any set-off provisions

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Finance Act 2005 amended Section 43(5) to exclude exchange-traded derivative transactions from the definition of speculative transactions. Therefore, F&O gains/losses are treated as non-speculative business income. Losses can be set off against any non-speculative business income and carried forward for 8 years. **The Trap:** Before 2005, derivative transactions WERE treated as speculative (losses only against speculative income, 4-year carry forward). The 2005 amendment changed this fundamentally. **Cross-Exam Context:** All three series (I Ch9, IV Ch9, VIII Ch9) cover this classification. The treatment is identical across currency, interest rate, and equity derivatives on exchanges. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This classification applies to ALL exchange-traded derivatives — equity F&O, currency futures/options, interest rate futures. OTC derivative treatment may differ.
